name: Ghanaian Pidgin English
notes: Ghanaian Pidgin English, an English-lexifier West African pidgin/creole. DELIBERATE STUB: the grapheme inventory is NOT specified and must not be guessed. The language is written in the Latin script but has NO official or standardised orthography: APiCS Online (Huber, survey chapter 16) states plainly that 'there is no official orthography for Ghanaian Pidgin English, which is almost never used in print, but enjoys growing popularity in e-mails, chat groups, etc.' Writers use ad-hoc, English-etymological spellings, which are by definition not phonemic, so no cited letter-to-IPA table exists and none can be constructed without inventing one. The phonology itself IS described (Huber 2008, 'Ghanaian Pidgin English: phonology', in Varieties of English 4: Africa, South and Southeast Asia): 7-9 monophthongs with optional length and phonemic nasalisation, six diphthongs /ai au ɔi iɛ ɛa uɔ/, non-rhotic, with processes such as final-consonant devoicing and syllable simplification — but a phonology is not an orthography, and the mapping from ad-hoc English spelling to those phonemes is not recoverable. To lift this stub, an actual published GPE spelling standard with a letter-to-sound table is required (e.g. if a Naijá-SNO-style convention is ever adopted for Ghana); until then an honest stub is preferred over a fabricated English-like map.

sources.author: Huber, Magnus
sources.title: Ghanaian Pidgin English (Survey chapter 16)
sources.publisher: The Atlas of Pidgin and Creole Language Structures Online, Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology
sources.notes: Explicit statement that there is no official orthography for Ghanaian Pidgin English; it is almost never used in print. Basis for keeping this spec a stub.

sources.author: Huber, Magnus
sources.title: Ghanaian Pidgin English: phonology
sources.publisher: Varieties of English 4: Africa, South and Southeast Asia, Mouton de Gruyter
sources.notes: Segmental phonology (7-9 monophthongs, nasalisation, six diphthongs /ai au ɔi iɛ ɛa uɔ/, non-rhotic). Describes sounds, not a letter-to-sound mapping, so it cannot by itself yield a grapheme table.

sources.author: Holm, John
sources.title: Pidgins and Creoles, Volume II: Reference Survey
sources.notes: Reference survey of Atlantic and Pacific pidgins/creoles; lexifier and substrate attributions.

sources.author: Michaelis, S.M., Maurer, P., Haspelmath, M. & Huber, M. (eds.)
sources.title: The Atlas of Pidgin and Creole Language Structures (APiCS)
sources.notes: Structural atlas of 76 pidgins/creoles; per-language survey chapters cited in ancestry notes.

sources.author: Huber, Magnus
sources.title: Ghanaian Pidgin English in its West African Context
sources.notes: West African Pidgin English; Akan/Ga (Kwa) substrate.

location.notes: Glottolog's representative point for Ghanaian Pidgin English.

ancestors.notes: English lexifier; Ghanaian Pidgin English within the West African Pidgin English continuum. Huber (1999); APiCS ch. 17 (Huber).
Akan (Kwa) substrate: tone/prosody, 7-vowel tendency with ATR harmony, labial-velar stops /kp ɡb/. Ga (also a substrate) is unregistered. Huber (1999).
